module_category: General Skills: Orientation and Analyzing Behavior
title: Missing-Links Analysis

download a larger version of this worksheet from www.guilford.com/dbt-worksheets.
General Worksheet 3   (General Handout 8)
Missing-Links Analysis
To understand missing effective behavior, do a missing-links analysis.
Due Date:
  Name:                                       Date:
Missing Behavior:
Use this sheet to first figure out what got in the way of doing things you needed or hoped to do, or
things you agreed to do or others expected you to do. Then use that information to problem-solve,
so that you will be more likely to do what is needed, hoped for, or expected next time.
1. Did I know what effective behavior was needed or expected? Yes
 No
If no to Question 1, what got in the way of knowing?
Describe problem solving:
 STOP
2. If yes to Question 1, was I willing to do what was needed? Yes      No
If no to Question 2, what got in the way of wanting to do what was needed?
Describe problem solving:
 STOP
3. If yes to Question 2, did the thought of doing what was needed or expected ever enter my
mind? Yes      No
If no to Question 3, describe problem solving:
4. If yes to Question 3, what got in the way of doing what was needed or expected right away?
 STOP
Describe problem solving:
 STOP

39
Mindfulness Skills
Introduction to Handouts and Worksheets
Mindfulness is the act of consciously focusing the mind in the present moment,
without judgment and without attachment to the moment. A person who is mind-
ful is aware in and of the present moment. Mindfulness is the opposite of being on
“automatic pilot,” or being lost in habit. Mindfulness has to do with the quality of
awareness that a person brings to everyday living. It’s a way of living awake, with
eyes wide open. As a set of skills, mindfulness practice is the intentional process of
observing, describing, and participating in reality nonjudgmentally, in the moment,
and with effectiveness (i.e., using skillful means). We can contrast mindfulness with
rigidly clinging to the present moment, as if we could keep a present moment from
changing if we cling hard enough. When we are mindful, we are open to the fluidity
of each moment as it arises and falls away.
Goals and Definitions
•• Mindfulness Handout 1: Goals of Mindfulness Practice. The goals of practic-
ing mindfulness skills, for most people, are to reduce suffering, increase happiness,
and increase control of the mind. For some, a goal of mindfulness is to experience
reality as it is. Mindfulness skills require practice, practice, practice.
•• Mindfulness Handout 2: Mindfulness Definitions. This handout offers basic
definitions of mindfulness, mindfulness skills, and mindfulness practice.
•• Mindfulness Worksheet 1: Pros and Cons of Practicing Mindfulness. This
worksheet is designed to help you decide whether you have anything to gain from
practicing mindfulness.
Core Mindfulness Skills
The handouts and worksheets for Core Mindfulness Skills cover seven skills in three
sets: Wise Mind; the “what” skills of observing, describing, and participating; and
the “how” skills of practicing nonjudgmentally, one-mindfully, and effectively.

ourspace_shared_life_filter.title: Missing-Links Analysis
ourspace_shared_life_filter.shared_facing_summary: Use this as a no-blame map. The point is not who failed; the point is what barrier showed up and what support would make the next try smaller.
tiny_steps: Circle one link: body, brain, access, trauma cue, timing, or environment.
Choose one repair or prevention step.
Save the lesson; do not punish the person.

flare_day_versions.minimum: One breath, one label, one safe message, or one checkbox.
flare_day_versions.medium: Two to five minutes with everything reachable from bed/chair.
flare_day_versions.full: Use the original worksheet/handout when both bodies and brains have capacity.

accessibility_modifications: Do chain analysis verbally, by voice note, or with one checkbox.
Include pain, oxygen, MCAS, fatigue, dissociation, caregiver load, and mobility access as real links.
relationship_and_caregiver_notes: William's needs and Jasper's capacity both count.
Ask before helping; ask before escalating; name the smallest next step.
Pause means pause, not abandonment. I am coming back. I care about us. I need a smaller next step.
What is needed, what capacity exists, what can be smaller, and what can wait?
identity_and_trauma_filter: Use William's masculine language and Jasper's they/them language.
Use consent-based, non-shaming, body-neutral language unless a person has explicitly chosen a term.
Do not turn symptoms, dysphoria, trauma responses, clutter, fatigue, or emotional intensity into moral failure.
avoid_or_adjust: Do not use this as a courtroom, interrogation, or proof that someone did not try.
